coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are nanostructures containing mainly carbon atoms and abundant functional groups. With remarkable and adjustable physicochemical properties, CDs have excellent hydrophilicity, photoluminescence (PL), biocompatibility, and low toxicity. Although the numerous advantages make CDs a research target for synthesizing advanced materials, some limitations are pertinent and must be corrected. Rare earth elements (RE) are excellent candidates for doping CDs, obtaining hybrid materials called RE-CDs to optimize luminescence properties, applicability, and quantum yields. Hybrids allow the combination of the advantageous characteristics of both CDs and RE, drastically improving their luminous and magneto-optical imaging performance and opening the door to numerous practical and technological applications. To date, no studies in the literature have provided in-depth analyses of the methods used to prepare RE-CDs, the characterization techniques used, the challenges, and a critical analysis of what could be improved in the synthesis by proposing practical solutions. To fill this gap, this review initially presents a detailed survey of CDs and RE separately. Subsequently, RE-CDs hybrid materials are addressed, as well as their obtainment, commonly used characterizations, and recent applications, from analyte detection to their functionality in medical nanodevices. Finally, criticisms and suggestions for future work are also discussed to inspire new research and discoveries about the technological potential of hybrid materials derived from the doping of RE-CDs.

Comparison of RE-CDs Synthesis Methods: Advantages and Disadvantages.

row.entry:
• The method is relatively simple and easy to perform.
• The synthesis takes place in a short period.
• It can be applied to various carbon and rare earth precursors.

• Heating at high temperatures may result in material loss or unwanted decomposition.
• May have limited control over the properties of the synthesized RE-CDs.
• May lead to the formation of unwanted impurities in the RE-CDs.

• It allows precise control of the properties of RE-CDs, such as size, shape, and composition.
• Results in RE-CDs with high purity and low presence of impurities.
• It takes place at relatively low temperatures, which can be beneficial to avoid the decomposition of sensitive compounds.

• It requires special equipment, such as a pressure reactor, which can make the method more complex and expensive.
• Generally, it requires longer reaction times compared to other methods.
• Achieving high yields in hydrothermal synthesis can be challenging.

• The use of microwaves can accelerate the synthesis of RE-CDs, thus reducing the reaction time.
• They allow precise temperature control during synthesis, avoiding unwanted decomposition.
• It can result in RE-CDs of high purity and low impurity formation.

• It requires using a microwave oven, which may not be widely available.
• Inappropriate application of microwaves may result in overheating of the system, leading to undesired reactions.
• May be more suitable for small-scale syntheses, challenging for large-scale productions.

• The method is simple, requiring no complicated equipment or processes.
• It is a more economical approach as it requires fewer reagents and specialized equipment.
• It allows the tuning of the properties of CDs, such as size, composition, and luminescence, through the selection of carbon precursors, carbonization conditions, and added dopants.

• It can result in a wide variation in the particle size of the CDs and the synthesis yield.
• May result in residues or undesirable impurities in the synthesized CDs, affecting the purity and quality of the CDs and requiring additional purification steps.
• It usually involves using high temperatures to decompose the carbon precursors.

• Simple method, and easy to reproduce.
• Cost-effective method, and no complex equipment required.
• Fast synthesis.

• Limited control over size and shape.
• Less control over properties.
• Undesirable reactions may occur.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are nanostructures containing mainly carbon atoms and abundant functional groups. With remarkable and adjustable physicochemical properties, CDs have excellent hydrophilicity, photoluminescence (PL), biocompatibility, and low toxicity. Although the numerous advantages make CDs a research target for synthesizing advanced materials, some limitations are pertinent and must be corrected. Rare earth elements (RE) are excellent candidates for doping CDs, obtaining hybrid materials called RE-CDs to optimize luminescence properties, applicability, and quantum yields. Hybrids allow the combination of the advantageous characteristics of both CDs and RE, drastically improving their luminous and magneto-optical imaging performance and opening the door to numerous practical and technological applications. To date, no studies in the literature have provided in-depth analyses of the methods used to prepare RE-CDs, the characterization techniques used, the challenges, and a critical analysis of what could be improved in the synthesis by proposing practical solutions. To fill this gap, this review initially presents a detailed survey of CDs and RE separately. Subsequently, RE-CDs hybrid materials are addressed, as well as their obtainment, commonly used characterizations, and recent applications, from analyte detection to their functionality in medical nanodevices. Finally, criticisms and suggestions for future work are also discussed to inspire new research and discoveries about the technological potential of hybrid materials derived from the doping of RE-CDs.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon Dots (CDs) are carbon-based nanomaterials with a size of less than 10 nm and a generally spherical shape, which have attracted significant attention from the scientific community since their discovery in 2004 [1]. CDs have exceptional characteristics such as fluorescence properties, biocompatibility, versatility of synthesis from any organic precursor, low toxicity, abundance of functional groups, adjustable emission wavelength, photostability, effortless surface modification, and chemical inertness [2–6]. The CDs’ potential has been explored in various areas, including bioimaging, detection, selective sensors, effective adsorbents, cell tracking, medical diagnostic imaging, catalysis, energy storage, and solar cells, among others [7,8]. For this reason, the number of studies involving CDs is growing due to their peculiarities, making them the target of recent studies in the area [9–21].

Despite the numerous advantages of CDs, there are still challenges to overcome, such as a thorough understanding of their luminescence process, the great sensitivity of their triplet states to the external environment, and the fluorescence quenching mechanism [2,22,23]. Furthermore, in optoelectronic and biological applications, such as information encryption [24], cellular imaging [25], molecular sensors [26], and photocatalysis [27], among others, persistent phosphorescence at room temperature (RTP) is a property of great importance [28,29]. This property associated with the high phosphorescent quantum yield is a major challenge for CDs because CD-based RTP materials are limited to milliseconds of life [30–32]. In addition, the phosphorescence quantum yield is low (less than 10 %) [33]. This hinders applications involving lighting and displays, where it is necessary to increase the quantum efficiency of CDs, which measures the percentage of excited electrons that emit light [34]. Another challenge associated with CDs is control over particle size and dispersion. Due to their highly hydrophilic and hygroscopic nature, they absorb moisture easily and can form particle aggregates. In sensor applications, it is challenging to create CDs sensitive to certain targets, such as organic molecules, metal ions, or toxic compounds [35].

Strategies have therefore been developed to get around these limitations, including heteroatom doping and surface modifications [36]. Doping becomes an interesting method since traditional techniques of preparing CDs restrict their functional groups to oxygen-containing groups, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [37]. This limits the detection sites on the surface of the CDs, resulting in low selectivity and sensitivity [38]. Numerous efforts have been made to increase the functionality of CDs, including inserting another element into the structure, known as doping [39]. Elements such as nitrogen, phosphorus, boron, and sulfur are the most commonly used for this purpose, closely related to increasing the quantum yield of CDs, as discussed in recent works [33,40–47]. The use of different atoms for doping, especially rare earth elements (RE), has been widely explored [3,48].

In this sense, combining CDs with rare earth elements is a promising strategy to form a hybrid RE-CDs material. The reason for using RE as a dopant is to promote greater luminescence stability. Due to their strong coordination properties and convenient interaction with surface functional groups on CDs, RE ions have been widely used to dope CDs [49]. This combination is possible due to CDs' easy and adjustable binding with the RE+3 ion, making them ideal for composing the hybrid [50]. The distinct qualities and possible applications of these hybrid materials are strongly influenced by their chemical structures and synthesis processes. Therefore, understanding the properties, synthesis methods, and, especially, the limitations of CDs and RE are crucial.

The review articles in this field have explored the subject from various perspectives, investigating functionalities, fluorescence mechanisms, doping, and surface functionalization of carbon dots, without delving into hybrid materials involving rare earth-doped carbon dots [58]. Zheng et al. [59] focus on doping with rare earth elements in the context of inorganic materials, while Gupta et al. [60] concentrate on optical nanomaterials, specifically rare earth-doped oxides. Additionally, Zhu et al. [61] present a review of rare earth-doped up-conversion nanoparticles, and recent progress in the field. Since 2020, there has been a significant surge of over 60 % in scientific publications on carbon dots doped with rare earth. The emphasis has primarily been on the applications of these materials. Despite these excellent works [59–62], there is a notable gap in the literature regarding the lack of comprehensive reviews on the combination of carbon dots and rare earth, addressing their applications, synthesis methods, and concepts related to both materials.

This review study aims to fill this gap by proposing an in-depth study of both materials (CDs and RE). Firstly, CDs' general aspects, important concepts, synthesis methods, and limitations are revisited. Subsequently, an in-depth review of rare earth elements, concepts, definitions, occurrence, optical and electronic aspects, and extraction methods is presented. After this introductory section, the aspects related to rare earth-doped carbon dots hybrid materials are addressed, focusing on the synthesis and applications of the main RE. An overview of the most recent published studies is discussed. As a differential not found in other studies, the main limitations in synthesizing RE-CDs are critically discussed, gaps that need improvement are presented, and practical strategies for enhancing the synthesis are provided. In addition, an in-depth discussion of the main techniques used to characterize RE-CDs, focusing on the most recent publications, is presented. Finally, a reflection on the main perspectives related to the topic is proposed, identifying gaps in the literature with a potential to be explored. With the examples, details, and recent hybrid materials applications, this review could stimulate new work and scale-up investigations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots: definitions and synthesis methods
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon Dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional nanomaterials smaller than 10 nm, spherical in shape, and have excellent photoluminescence. CDs were discovered accidentally in 2004 in a study involving the synthesis of carbon nanotubes, defined as cylindrical carbon structures of nanometric size [1]. During this study, the formation of tiny carbon particles with remarkable optical properties was accidentally observed. The definition of CDs was presented two years after its discovery, in 2006, and is used to date [63].

Unlike traditional quantum dot semiconductors, the structure of CDs has sp2 hybridized (graphitized) carbon in its inner core. Their exterior is rich in functional groups with nitrogen and oxygen atoms in different forms [64,65]. The presence of oxygenated functional groups on the surface favors the dispersion of nanoparticles in water, enhancing their application in other areas [66]. Compared to conventional semiconductors, an advantage of CDs is that the latter have properties favorable to various applications, such as high dispersibility, high biocompatibility, low toxicity, and high photostability [4,5]. The biocompatibility and cytotoxicity of CDs are essential characteristics for biological applications [66]. CDs have lower cytotoxicity when compared to quantum dots that have heavy metals in their structure, and toxicity varies at different concentrations, molecular designs, and sizes [66,67]. CDs present other core structures and surface configurations resulting from the various carbon sources and synthesis routes. CDs can usually be classified into four categories: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [4,5,68]. Commonly referred to as CDs, carbon nanopowders are the most frequently used materials and are usually obtained from alternative carbon sources.

The main routes for CD synthesis employ bottom-up and top-down approaches (Fig. 1 ) [69]. The bottom-up route has been the most widely used because it utilizes simple processes, has low cost, and has better control over the final material properties [70]. Bottom-up methods convert small organic molecules into CDs through chemical reactions [71,72] favoring biomass as a precursor, as most are composed of small organic molecules that can be carbonized at high temperatures [71]. The bottom-up approach involves methods such as thermal decomposition [73,74], hydrothermal carbonization [75,76], solvothermal carbonization, and microwave irradiation [77,78], with great control over the final properties of CDs, in particular, the shape and size of the nanomaterial [79,80].

Thermal decomposition is an endothermic process by which a substance or compound chemically decomposes under the influence of heat in a common thermal decomposition. It is a low-cost process that uses high temperatures to decompose carbon precursors. The synthesis process is easy to handle operationally, is feasible to scale-up, and allows fast processing [81]. Several studies have reported synthesis by this route, using citric acid via pyrolysis at 180 °C for 20 h [82] or glucose as a precursor at 200 °C for 12 h [83]. Collagen was also used as a carbon source treated at 350 °C for 2 h at a heating rate of 5 °C/min to synthesize CDs and apply them to Fe3+ ion detection and fluorescent imaging [84]. However, these studies involve long pyrolysis times, a parameter that could be optimized. For this reason, a recent study used fast pyrolysis of 15 min to 20 min at 200 °C using citric acid, urea, and polyethyleneimine as precursors to synthesize CDs and apply them to the photocatalytic degradation of the organic dye rhodamine B [85]. In addition, Wang et al. [86] proposed an ultra-fast synthesis of just 1 min at 240 °C to synthesize CDs with a quantum yield of 47 % [86]. Despite the advantages of this route, the uniformity and precision control of the material's particle size and final properties are fragile points that merit investigation [87].

Recently, one-step solvothermal carbonization was used in the presence of citric acid and urea in dimethylformamide at 166 °C for 11 h and 24 min [88], producing colored carbon quantum dots for application in white light-emitting diodes. On the other hand, another study used o-phenylenediamine as a precursor and water as a solvent in a hydrothermal synthesis at 200 °C for 10 h, obtaining CDs with a high fluorescence quantum yield (41.0 %) [89]. Although both require long synthesis times, these methods make it possible to synthesize CDs with a high QY [87]. In addition, it is possible to adjust material properties such as particle dispersion, crystallinity, and purity according to adjustments in temperature, pressure, and type of solvent, for example. These aspects, along with practicality, low impact, and the possibility of using various types of precursors, are important advantages of carbonization methods [90].

Finally, microwave irradiation synthesizes CDs by rapidly heating the organic precursors, which decompose during the process and become CDs. The synthesis occurs quickly and efficiently, with uniform particle distribution and easy handling [87]. Shibata et al. [91] used starch as a precursor in a synthesis heated in a microwave oven (250 W) for 2–14 min, obtaining CDs with a high QY (30 %) [34]. Ultra-fast syntheses of just 3.5 min using microwaves at 490 W were carried out, obtaining CDs with a QY of 94.4 % with great efficiency in detecting hydrogen peroxide [92]. In addition, other recent studies have used this route to make CDs applied to compound detection [93], white light emitting diodes and anti-counterfeiting applications [94], and adsorption of contaminants [95], among others.

On the other hand, the top-down approach refers to the breakdown of large carbon framework molecules (graphite, graphene, carbon nanotubes, and activated carbon) into smaller units using harsh conditions that provide less control over the process and, consequently, over the chemical, physical and optical properties of the CDs [81]. This approach uses chemical oxidation, laser ablation, and arc discharge, which employ light, electricity, heat, and chemistry to break down molecules [79,80,96].

Chemical oxidation is characterized by using strong oxidizing acids (sulfuric and nitric acid, among others) to oxidize and peel the precursor, promoting the conversion of organic molecules into CDs [97]. This technique has advantages such as low cost, higher synthesis yield, purity, and adjustable size control [98]. Recent studies in the literature have used concentrated phosphoric acid, phosphinic acid, sulphuric acid, and hydrogen peroxide combined with sodium hydroxide as oxidizers for this purpose [98–103]. The size of the nanoparticles can be adjusted through the concentration of acid used and thus influence the photoluminescent properties of the final product [104]. Despite the advantages and easy adjustment of the final properties, there are still points that need to be rethought and improved in this method. For example, using toxic and corrosive chemical reagents requires careful handling and safety measures. In addition, impurities can be deposited in the mixture, requiring additional steps to remove the acid and residuals, making the process laborious [99].

Ultrasound is another top-down technique that uses high-energy ultrasonic waves that can fractionate large carbon molecules. It is applied to a solvent and organic precursor mixture, causing fragmentation and conversion into smaller CDs [81]. Recently nitrogen-doped multicolored CDs were synthesized using ultrasonic treatment of kiwi juice with different additive reagents such as ethanol, ethylenediamine, and acetone. The synthesis was carried out using high-intensity ultrasonic irradiation (320 W, 80 kHz) at room temperature (RT) for 3 h. The process proved simple, reproducible, and effective in fluorescent dyes and fluorescent probes for detecting Fe3+ ions, phosphate anion (PO4 3−), and ascorbic acid [105]. Dried Polyalthia longifolia leaves in distilled water were recently sonicated for 1 h to synthesize CDs using a practical and reproducible method [106]. The ultrasound method has been used in other recent works in the literature because it is easy to reproduce and can potentially be invested in [106–109]. Compared to the previous method, ultrasound has a lower environmental impact and advantages associated with accessibility and low cost. However, it is still necessary to overcome the limitation of uneven heating of the samples, related to the local thermal effect of ultrasonic waves, which reduces the effectiveness of the reaction when compared to direct heating or microwave treatment [99].

Finally, there is the laser ablation method, where a high-energy laser beam is focused on the surface of a solid object, i.e., the carbon precursor, which is submerged in a solution [110]. Advantages such as adjustable properties due to precise laser control and synthesis carried out shortly can be mentioned. The final material is directly dependent on the initial state of the precursor (solid target or immersed in liquid). CDs were synthesized after laser ablation of graphite in powder form, in which the size and properties of the final product can be adjusted according to the spot size, irradiation time, and laser power. As an advantage, it is possible to obtain ultra-small CDs by increasing the irradiation time [111]. Other recent works have also been inspired by this route for making CDs [112–114].

The top-down approach preserves the graphene structure of the carbon materials [68]. It allows the use of small-sized residual carbon materials that can be transformed into CDs [71]. However, the quantum yield of the product is lower than that of CDs obtained by the bottom-up approach [79,115]. More details on the synthesis routes will be discussed in Section 4.

CDs present several interesting peculiarities that justify their broad applicability. CDs exhibit strong absorption in the ultraviolet (UV) region, which extends into the visible region [116,117]. The absorption bands are related to the transition π-π* of the link CC or the transition n−π* of the link CO/CN [66,67,115]. Due to the presence of oxygen and nitrogen, as well as surface defects, variations in the position of the absorption peaks may occur [66,67]. Different synthesis processes produce CDs with other optical absorption characteristics [115]. In addition to the molecular structure, external effects such as the solvent used in the synthesis process make CDs with different optical properties, especially optical absorption characteristics [68].

Another crucial property for the application of CDs is photoluminescence, although its origin in CDs still needs to be fully clarified in the literature [66]. Fluorescent intensity is quantitatively measured by quantum yield. It is directly affected by internal and external factors such as using different synthesis approaches, precursors, solvents, reaction temperature, conjugated and unconjugated small molecules, and post-treatment methods [118]. The internal factors affecting photoluminescence are the quantum confinement effect, surface state, and synergistic effect [68].

The quantum confinement effect refers to the size of the domain sp2. Larger graphene domain "sizes" in the core of CDs relate to larger displacements, with a more uniform energy distribution, which decreases the energy gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) [67]. Fluorescence in most CDs shows greater dependence on chemical composition and structure than quantum size, indicating the strong relationship between photoluminescence and surface state [4,119]. The surface state mechanism explains the light changes corresponding to changes in the electronic energy levels. These changes can be caused by surface configuration and doping with heteroatoms. Regarding the surface configurations, functional groups are responsible for introducing fluorophores with different energy levels into the CDs, changing the emission centers, and eventually adjusting the fluorescence [68,71,79]. In addition to the functional groups, surface defects serve as excitation centers, causing fluorescence related to the surface state of the CDs. In this case, the higher the degree of oxidation of the surface of the CDs, the higher the number of defects on the surface, causing a shift to the red region at the emission wavelength.

Finally, another highlight is the synergistic effect that unites the individualities of each model to describe the photoluminescence mechanisms better. Among the synergistic effects reported are the combination of the impact of the carbon core and the surface state, the conjugation of the carbon core and the functional groups present on the surface, and the conjugation effect and the surface defects [68].

Carbon dots have vast potential thanks to their unique properties, such as high stability, chemical versatility, and light emission capacity. Among the different fields of application of CDs, their use in the biomedical and environmental areas and photocatalysis as a photocatalyst and photosensitizer stands out [120] and as an analyte sensor [121]. In the biomedical area, they are used as biosensors to diagnose diseases and as biomarkers for in vivo and in vitro bioimaging tests [122]. Due to their low toxicity and high photoluminescence, the application of CDs as sensors has been widely investigated [123–130]. CDs have been used as fluorescent probes to detect analytes such as chemical pollutants and biologically active small molecules [131]. The advantages of using CDs as fluorescent probes include low cost compared to conventional detection techniques, low production cost, rapid response, and in situ detection [64,115]. The functional groups present on the surface make the CDs reactive to the environment where they are inserted, interacting with metal ions, and forming a new electronic combination, thus altering their optical properties to increase or extinguish the fluorescence in the presence of the analytes [16,23,36,48]. Typically, CDs have been used for the detection of heavy metals in the form of ions such as Fe3+ [132–134], Cu2+ [123–125] and Hg2+ [128].

In summary, carbon dots stand out as a promising class of nanomaterials due to their fascinating properties and diverse potential applications. Studying these nanomaterials is an exciting and promising field with a vast potential for future discoveries. Significant challenges, such as the lack of a precise definition and a systematic classification, open space for further studies [135]. The relationship between the carbon core, functional groups, and possible surface modifiers also represents a promising field to drive the development of carbon dots with novel and tailored functionalities and abilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rare earth: definitions, occurrence, and extraction methods
ce:section.ce:para: Rare earth elements (RE) are a group of 17 elements comprising the elements scandium (Sc), yttrium (Y), and the 15 called lanthanides, with the same electrical ground state configuration [136]. A timeline of the discoverers of rare earth elements is presented in Fig. 2 . The lanthanides group includes the following elements: lanthanum (La), cerium (Ce), praseodymium (Pr), neodymium (Nd), promethium (Pm), samarium (Sm), europium (Eu), gadolinium (Gd), terbium (Tb), dysprosium (Dy), holmium (Ho), erbium (Er), thulium (Th), ytterbium (Yb) and lutetium (Lu) [137]. Promethium (Pm) is the only one that does not occur naturally. Yttrium and scandium are rare earth elements due to their geochemical behavior similar to the lanthanides [138].

The RE elements are commonly found in the trivalent oxidation state (RE3+), but europium (Eu) can also be found in the divalent state (Eu2+) and cerium (Ce4+) in the tetravalent state [139]. Although they are relatively numerous in the planet's overall composition, they are considered "rare" because they are not frequently discovered in large concentrations in the Earth's crust [140]. Nevertheless, they can be found in higher abundance in soils, rocks, minerals, and plants; the rarity designation of these elements can be considered outdated [141,142]. Based on several physicochemical characteristics, such as atomic number, electronic configuration, and relative abundance in the Earth's crust, RE can be categorized as light, heavy, or medium [143–145].

Rare earth elements occur in minerals formed and found in the Earth's crust. They can appear as primary constituent elements of minerals, as impurities in minerals, or adsorbed on mineral particles. In the first form of occurrence, RE is found in the crystalline network of minerals as ionic compounds or mineral components and can be called rare earth minerals, such as monazite ([Ce, La, Th, Nd, Y]PO4), bastnaesite (CeCO3F), and xenotime (YPO4) [146].

Although they are found in various geological formations around the world [147], the occurrence of rare earth elements is not homogeneous, and some countries concentrate on a greater production of these elements [148]. The major countries holding rare earth include China, Brazil, Vietnam, Russia, and India (Fig. 3 ), comprising 130 million tons of rare earth deposits [149].

These materials come from four types of geological environments: carbonatites, alkaline igneous systems, ion-adsorbed clay deposits, and placer deposits containing monazite-xenotime. China is the leading producer of rare earth oxides (REO), concentrating approximately 97 % of mining, concentration, separation, and refining of REO [150], and is a leader in rare earth exploration and production, accounting for one-third of all RE deposits. In addition, other factors such as low production and labor costs, large mining operations, abundant and extensive RE reserves, a vertically integrated supply chain from extraction to RE refining and production, and export restrictions reinforce the current Chinese monopoly on RE production [151,152].

RE elements are used in various applications, as summarized in Fig. 4 . Some of the most significant applications can be briefly cited: ceramic materials, metal alloys, fuels, microwaves, temperature sensors, computer monitors, batteries, catalysts in the oil refining industry, automotive batteries, high technology such as computer batteries, X-ray films, lasers, digital cameras, polishing for glass, mirrors, microprocessors for hard disks, pigments, photographic films, high-powered magnets for computers, electric motors, fluorescent lighting, glass additives, general communication devices, contrast agent for magnetic resonance imaging, fiber optic technology, and solar panels [153].

A significant drawback of producing rare earth elements is the environmental impact of this activity on nature [154]. The process involves considerable energy expenditure, chemicals, and waste generation, such as waste gases, acidified water, and radioactive waste containing uranium or thorium [77]. As the mining step is crucial for RE extraction and production, it is essential to address the sustainability challenges of mineral resources since recycling these elements would reduce the environmental impact and risks associated with RE disposal [154,155].

The extraction and separation of rare earth elements involve several processes, from mining to obtaining the individual elements, which can be separated into mining, beneficiation, chemical extraction, purification, and refining steps [156]. Among the main methods used for separating and purifying RE are chemical extraction, acid decomposition, alkaline decomposition, oxidation by roasting, decomposition by chlorination, and membrane separation [157–160]. In chemical extraction, rare earth minerals are transformed into soluble and easily extractable forms. Different acids, such as sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, and hydrofluoric acid, are mineral-degrading agents [161–163]. In alkaline decomposition, the minerals are decomposed by reacting with sodium hydroxide [164].

Besides, rare earth ores are transformed into rare earth oxides through roasting oxidation, increasing their solubility and making extracting and purifying them easier. To encourage the oxidation of rare earth elements, this approach entails carefully heating the ore at high temperatures in the presence of oxygen [165–167]. Another method is chlorination decomposition, which occurs by reacting rare earth minerals with chlorine at different temperatures [168,169]. In addition to chemical processes, rare earth separation can be performed using membranes. The membrane separation technique combines extraction and removal simultaneously without thermal processes such as distillation, drying, or evaporation [170]. This approach is more sustainable as it consumes less energy and uses fewer volatile organic chemicals. However, the stability of these membranes still represents a challenge for their application on an industrial scale [171]. Finally, separating RE using solvent extraction is considered the most practical, efficient, and economical way to obtain individual rare earth elements of high purity [172]. Several chemical reagents with extraction properties can be used in this process, e.g., 2-ethyl(hexyl)phosphoric acid-mono-2-ethylhexyl ester (HEH(EHP) or P507); di(2-ethylhexyl)phosphoric acid (HDEHP or P204); Cyanex272 (trihexyltetradecylphosphonium); primary amine N1923 and Trialkylamine N235 [173–176].

Rare earth elements exhibit a wide range of interesting electronic and optical properties. The high magnetic field strength and optical and luminescent properties of RE result from the electronic transitions that occur in the 4f layer, together with the high atomic number, which allows for a diversity of energy levels and electronic transitions [177]. The electronic configuration of the rare earth elements varies according to atomic number, with the 4f layer being characteristically filled, based on the configuration of xenon [Xe]4fn6s2 and [Xe]4fn-15d16s2 with n varying between 1 and 14. For the element scandium (Z = 21), its ground state configuration is based on argon, [Ar]3d14s2, while the element yttrium (Z = 39) has an electron configuration based on krypton [Kr]4d15s2 [7].

One of the leading optical characteristics of RE is the presence of very close electronic energy levels, which results in highly defined electronic transitions and the absorption and emission of light over a wide range of wavelengths [178]. This property is exploited in applications of luminescent materials such as fluorescent lamps, plasma television screens, and light-emitting diode (LED) devices [179]. Each rare earth ion has characteristic electronic energy levels, emitting light in specific colors [180].

Luminescence, light absorption, and emission are phenomena resulting from the electronic transitions of electrons in the partially filled 4f layer of rare earth elements. Luminescence involves different mechanisms, such as fluorescence, phosphorescence, chemiluminescence, bioluminescence, and electroluminescence [181]. Fluorescence is the most common type of luminescence, in which light absorption at a specific wavelength occurs, followed by light emission at a longer wavelength [182]. Phosphorescence is similar to fluorescence, but the excited state of the electron is prolonged, resulting in light emission even in the absence of an excitation [183]. Chemiluminescence, in turn, is a phenomenon in which the emission of electromagnetic radiation occurs at specific wavelengths in the visible and near-infrared spectrum region. This happens due to a redox reaction that generates unstable intermediates in excited states. These intermediates lose energy in the form of photons, resulting in the emission of light [184]. On the other hand, electroluminescence involves light emission by applying a current or electric field to a material, such as in light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [185,186].

The luminescence mechanisms arise from half-filled 4f layer electronic transitions. Elements such as scandium [Ar] 4s²3d¹, yttrium [Kr] 4d15s2, lanthanum [Xe] 5d¹6 s and lutetium [Xe] 4f145d16s2 have the 4f layer filled or do not have electrons in the 4f layer and, for this reason, do not have electronic levels capable of promoting the phenomenon of excitation and emission of luminescence [187]. In contrast, elements between cerium and ytterbium have partially filled 4f sublevels, giving them a large diversity of characteristic energy levels and electronic transition dynamics, justifying their luminescence mechanism in the UV–Vis and infrared region [188]. Lanthanide ions also exhibit narrow emission bands, large Stokes shifts, and a prolonged duration of excited states [2].

The stability of the rare earth elements is related to the electronic configuration of the 4f orbitals. The energy levels of rare earth elements come from their characteristic electronic arrangement. When filled, the outermost subshells 5s2 and 5p6 act to shield the electrons in the 4f layer, making them less accessible to surrounding atoms or molecules [2]. This is because the partially filled electrons of the 4f orbitals are more attracted to the atom's nucleus. This characteristic favors the formation of stable compounds. It causes the electrons of rare earth elements to have an excellent affinity for highly electronegative chemical species such as oxygen and fluorine, covalently bonding with these elements [189]. This feature represents the main difference between inner transition elements and outer transition metals, whose 3d electrons in the outermost shell are strongly affected by the chemical environment or by ligand field interactions [190].

Combining these elements with carbon dots to prepare hybrid materials has received notorious attention from the scientific community in recent years due to the knowledge of rare earth's electronic and optical properties and the promising potential of these elements. This approach becomes particularly interesting due to the luminescent properties (PL) also found in the carbon dots [191].

Doping with heteroatoms is carried out to modify the surface state of CDs, i.e., changing their structural, chemical, and electronic properties. In the literature, nitrogen, phosphorus, boron, sulfur, and other dopants are commonly used to increase the quantum yield and luminescent properties [36]. However, rare earth elements have incredible characteristics that make them promising. It is believed that the union of these two materials in forming the RE-CDs hybrid is a very valuable strategy to boost new studies [3]. Before delving into the formation of RE-CDs, it is first necessary to understand the promising characteristics that make RE excellent candidates for doping CDs. Over the last three decades, luminous rare earth complexes have been widely researched for electroluminescence due to their clear emission and theoretical internal quantum efficiency of 100 %. Organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) are a common example, and their remarkable chemical, catalytic, electromagnetic, magnetic, and optical properties encourage their combination with CDs [185].

Due to the intrinsic 4f-4f electronic configurations of rare earth metals, complexes with RE have abundant orbital energy levels and fluorescent emissions with various distinct properties, making these compounds promising for applications such as fluorescent probes, among others [179]. The insertion of RE into the CDs’ structure has the potential to overcome a major limitation related to quantum yield. RE has excellent optical properties, high quantum yields, high electronic density, and discontinuous energy levels resulting from their unique 4f electronic configurations. For example, Eu3+ complexes with a characteristic 5d-4f transition have superior fluorescent properties with red-shifted emissions. Studies by Rosa et al. [192] proved the excellent ability of europium complexes to detect citrate at extracellular concentrations of up to 500 μM [192].

In addition, RE elements have remarkable magnetic properties, such as gadolinium, which makes them suitable for magnetic detection and as contrast agents with excellent biocompatibility and data storage [193–195]. According to Zhang et al. [196], gadolinium is one of the rare earth elements with the highest electron spin magnetic moment, which allows it to improve the signal intensity of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agents by shortening the relaxation period of T1-weighted images [196]. Due to their large transition numbers, which cover a wide range of wavelengths from the near ultraviolet to the infrared, rare earth elements are often used [3]. In addition, due to their electrical conduction properties, they can be used to dope semiconductor CDs, altering their electrical conduction properties and allowing them to be used to create transistors and sensors [197].

Therefore, it is possible to list some advantages of RE-CDs hybrids, such as more selective binding sites than pure CDs, greater luminescent stability, and greater water solubility than simple RE composites [50]. For this reason, combining both materials to form the RE-CDs hybrid is a more interesting strategy than using them isolated. RE-CDs hybrids also offer the advantage of preserving the luminescence of the CDs and the RE3+ ions and promoting an efficient interaction for potential use in several areas, generating greater sensitivity and precision, like ratiometric probes and others [36]. Moreover, the association of RE and CDs produces compounds with more intense and adjustable emissions that allow precise tracking of cells and biomolecules, with applications in imaging and sensing [198].

As previously reported, carbon dots have aroused interest in several research areas because they offer unique light-emitting properties, are environmentally friendly, have low toxicity, and have broad application potential. In addition, the great interest in the optical properties of CDs has turned the attention of researchers to the use of alternatives to increase the functionalities of this material and improve its optical, chemical, and electrical properties [36]. This prominence has driven research towards developing relatively simple synthesis routes in which nanomaterials can be combined or doped with other elements to enhance their properties or create optoelectronic properties suited to specific needs. Doping with heteroatoms is carried out to change the surface state of CDs, i.e., to alter their structural, chemical, and electronic properties, increasing their fluorescence intensity and fluorescence quantum yield [199]. All these benefits can be obtained by associating RE with the structure of CDs, forming the RE-CDs hybrid material [7].

The use of rare earth combined with carbon dots still needs to be explored in the literature. A search for the keywords "carbon dots", AND "rare earth" was conducted in June 2023 on the journal search platform Dimensions. One hundred twenty-eight papers were found that addressed a variety of carbon dots precursor materials, different relationships with rare earth elements, synthesis methods, and applications. Based on this list, the main synthesis methods and relevant applications were summarized, the latter topic being presented in Section 5 of this paper. RE-CDs are usually made by hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, or carbonization methods, and these techniques can be combined with additional steps of mixing, sol-gel method, and others. The produced materials can be further incorporated into host, inorganic, or polymeric matrices, aiming at versatile design ideas and applications. The one-step synthesis is the most effective and commonly used strategy for preparing RE-CDs as it preserves the luminescent, magnetic, and optical characteristics of the CDs and RE elements [3].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal and solvothermal synthesis of RE-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrothermal carbonization and solvothermal carbonization use high temperature and pressure conditions to synthesize carbon-based materials [200]. Hydrothermal carbonization uses water as a solvent, where the reactants and precursors are dissolved to carry out the reaction [79,118]. Solvothermal carbonization, on the other hand, dissolves the precursors and reagents in organic solvents such as ethanol, acetone, and others instead of water [201,202]. The challenges associated with these methods are the high energy demand due to the need for high temperatures to satisfy the carbonization [203].

The hydrothermal method is widely reported in the literature for synthesizing CDs and involves the reaction of carbon precursors in an aqueous solution under high temperatures and controlled pressures. The combination of heat and pressure during the hydrothermal process improves the digestion efficiency of the organic materials, and the sample can be heated longer without losing volume through evaporation [204]. Wang et al. [128] listed several advantages that make this the most widely used synthesis method. Firstly, the setup is simple and easy to handle. In addition, the process is suitable for different starting materials, such as small molecules, polymers, and biomass, among others. Another advantage is that the synthesized CDs show high product yield and high quantum yields. So, it is possible to dope other elements and adjust the properties of the CDs in a diverse way using this method. Due to this last feature, this method is widely applied in synthesizing RE-CDs.

As already reported, synthesis via the hydrothermal method can be performed in one-pot reactions or by applying approaches that require more than one step. For example, it is possible to prepare CDs and RE (or other RE-derived materials) separately and then combine them to form RE-CDs composite materials. Xu et al. [205] prepared NaYF4:Eu@CDs composites from the mixture of NaYF4:Eu nanoparticles and CDs. The CDs were prepared via a hydrothermal route in a Teflon-coated stainless-steel autoclave heated at 180 °C for 12 h. N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methyldimethoxy silane and anhydrous citric acid were the carbon precursor materials to obtain the CDs. NaYF4:Eu nanoparticles were also prepared by reaction in a stainless steel autoclave using NaCl as sodium precursor, ammonium fluoride as fluoride precursor, and Y3+ and Eu3+ salts to provide the rare earth ions.

Another approach for synthesizing RE-CDs composite materials applies the previously prepared CDs as one of the raw materials to prepare RE-based materials, thus forming RE-CDs composite materials. He et al. [191] synthesized ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs composites by the hydrothermal method in two steps: in the first one, CDs were prepared using o-phenylenediamine as a precursor in an autoclave at 130 °C for 2 h. ZBH:Tm3+/yNCDs composites were obtained by a simple hydrothermal reaction with boric acid (H3BO3), zinc vitriol (ZnSO4·7H2O), ammonia water (NH3·H2O), and thulium oxide (Tm2O3). The obtained sol-gel solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined reactor, and after the reaction at 180 °C for 72 h, the solid powders of the composite were obtained. A summarized schematic flowchart of the hydrothermal process of synthesis of RE-CDs based on the study developed by Thakur et al. [206] is presented in Fig. 5 .

One-pot synthesis is generally considered more attractive than multi-step synthesis due to the simplicity of the process, as all reagents required for synthesizing CDs are mixed and reacted in a single vessel or system. Single-step synthesis can increase the efficiency and yield of the reaction as it avoids the loss of intermediate reagents during inter-step transfer and reduces the possibility of unwanted contaminations. Chang et al. [207] synthesized nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) and Eu/Gd co-doped hydroxyapatite Eu/Gd (HA:Eu,Gd) via a one-step hydrothermal method. Sodium citrate was used as a carbon source for synthesizing CDs, diethylenetriamine was used as a nitrogen precursor, and gadolinium (Gd) was introduced to the material to intensify the red emission of europium (Eu). Another Tb-CQDs composite was prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method, with hydrated terbium nitrate as the Tb source and citric acid as the carbon precursor [208]. However, it is essential to emphasize that the choice between "one-pot" and multi-step synthesis depends on the specific characteristics of the reaction system, the desired properties of the CDs, technical limitations, and available resources. Each synthesis method has advantages and disadvantages, and the appropriate selection should be made based on the specific needs and objectives of the study or application.

Recently, europium-doped carbon dots (Eu-CDs) were synthesized using the hydrothermal method to determine metanil yellow dye in turmeric powder [209]. Tannic acid (TA) was used as the precursor, and europium (III) chloride hexahydrate as the rare earth metal provider [209]. Due to the presence of polyphenol groups in its skeleton, tannic acid (TA) can form a polyphenol-metal complex with metals acting as polydentate ligands [210]. The reaction mechanism took place in two stages. Firstly, the complex between TA and the europium ions, TA-Eu3+, was formed, and then the TA-Eu3+ complex was hydrothermally carbonized. The tannic acid was mixed with europium (III) chloride hexahydrate and stirred magnetically for 12 h, and 20 mL of the solution was treated at 220 °C for 7 h. The mixture was centrifuged, and the supernatant was filtered using a 0.45 µm membrane, obtaining the Eu-CDs [209]. MET images show agglomerated particles with an average size of 6 nm, compatible with literature findings. An interesting difference in this work was using an alternative precursor source, tannic acid from plants with abundant hydroxyl and phenolic groups [209]. Plants containing organic compounds with abundant functional groups, glucose, and cellulose are good substitutes for the synthetic chemical reagents used as precursors. However, as the fluorometric test was carried out to detect the dye, the quantum yield of the europium-doped CDs and those without the dopant could have been analyzed to assess the influence on the final photoluminescence.

Wang et al. [211] synthesized CDs@CePO4:Tb3+ nanocomposites prepared from the mixture of CDs and CePO4:Tb3+ nanorods. The CDs were prepared by a solvothermal method using 1,2,4,5-benzenetetracarboxylic acid and dimethylformamide as starting materials. Then, Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Tb(NO3)3·6H2O were mixed, and different amounts of CDs were added to the solution, stirred for some time, and transferred to a Teflon-coated stainless steel reactor, where they were heated at 170 °C for 10 h to obtain the RE-CDs composite. The preparation of a composite of CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ through a hydrothermal approach is described in the study by Li et al. [212]. The CDs were synthesized using citric acid as a carbon source in the presence of ethylenediamine. The synthesis of the composite CDs@YBO3:Eu3+ was carried out from the reaction in an autoclave heated at 180 °C for 26 h, using the solution of CDs, Y2O3, Eu2, O3, and HBO3 as raw material. Yi et al. [213] prepared CDs in a Teflon reactor using citric acid and urea as precursor materials. These CDs were used to synthesize a Eu-MOFs composite from the mixture of solutions of CDs, Eu(NO3)3·6H2O, anhydrous sodium acetate, and benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylic acid.

CDs with blue emission were obtained by the hydrothermal method at 200 °C for 8 h using citric acid and urea as the organic precursor. Subsequently, CDs were doped with different lanthanides using the in-situ method to prepare composites with integrated properties of different components for application in cryptography and information security. Color-adjustable up-conversion luminescence was achieved using the elements europium, ytterbium, and thulium. Red emissions were obtained in the presence of europium, and blue, green, and red color emissions were obtained through energy transfer between Er3+, Tm3+, and Yb3+ [214]. Hydrothermal synthesis has also been used in other relevant works that can be mentioned [215–218].

Another way of synthesizing hybrids is to use ready-made CDs and add the dopant at high temperatures to adhere to the structure. The method involves carbonizing carbon precursors by applying moderate to high temperatures in a controlled atmosphere. This method is relatively simple and low-cost and can be carried out in a heating mantle or a conventional furnace. However, only some studies have reported this technique for synthesizing RE-CDs. In their study, Dong et al. [219] obtained CDs between 3 nm and 5 nm in diameter by heating glycerol, diethylene glycol, and polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG400) in a single blanket for 1 h at 230 °C. The reaction with different rare earth metal salts (TbCl3·6H2O, EuCl3·6H2O) produced materials with intense blue and green emissions and a quantum yield of 50 %. The study conducted by Liu et al. [220] synthesized Rare-Earth Metal-Organic Frameworks (RE-MOFs) using Eu, Tb, Y, Dy, Er, or Tm. In this process, the precursors 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (H3BTC) and tetraethylammonium bromide (TEAB), the latter in different concentrations, were heated in an autoclave at 160 °C for 24 h.

Syntheses of RE-CDs by heating can also be conducted in more than one step. Luminescent aerogels with CDs containing ions Eu3+and Tb3+were prepared in a study conducted by Zhang et al. [221]. In the first step of the synthesis, CDs were synthesized from a mixture of citric acid and l-cysteine at 70 °C until complete drying. The residual material was heated in a stainless-steel autoclave equipped with Teflon at 200 °C for 3 h (Fig. 6 ). The second step consisted of preparing aerogels by adding Eu3+, Tb3+, and thymidine ions to the CDs by heating the mixture at 95 °C for 10 min. In the study by Lin and collaborators [222], a self-referential thermometry probe was prepared from a mixture of separately prepared CDs and YVO4:Eu3+ powders. The CDs were synthesized by heating EDA (ethylenediamine) and H3PO4 at 280 °C for 2 h in a quartz reactor, and the CDs/YVO4:Eu3+ composite was prepared by mixing both solid materials in equal proportions.

Recently, yttrium acetate tetrahydrate, ytterbium acetate hydrate, and thulium acetate hydrate were used as rare earth metal sources and mixed with CDs previously prepared hydrothermally at 180 °C for 8 h. To do this, 2 mg of the CD powder was mixed with the YF3:Yb,Tm precursors containing 3 mmol of rare earth acetates (78 % molar Y, 20 % molar Yb, and 2 % molar Tm). Doping was carried out via pyrolysis in a muffle furnace at 300 °C for 5 h, and the final hybrid material was designated as CDs@YF3:Yb,Tm. The CDs were incorporated into the YF3 matrix doped with ytterbium and thulium, in which YF3 played a dual role, acting as a host to attach the luminous rare earth centers and as a rigid matrix to stabilize the triple state of the CDs [223].

Wedelia Chinensis leaf extract was used as an alternative precursor to obtain CDs. CDs were synthesized using the reflux method with an aqueous solution of ammonium hydroxide as the nitrogen source and gadolinium hydroxide as the rare earth metal source. The CDs were first synthesized hydrothermally for 12 h at 180 °C using the extract and NH3. After the reaction, the nanoparticles were filtered using 0.22 μm membranes. Subsequently, 40 mL of the solution containing the CDs and 3 mg of gadolinium hydroxide were mixed and refluxed in an oil bath for 8 h and heated to 100 °C. After centrifuging, washing, and drying, the hybrid material called n-CDs@Gd(OH)3 was obtained. Co-doping with nitrogen and hydroxyl groups from gadolinium hydroxide improved its application in electrocatalysis [224]. However, no quantitative data was provided on the quantum yield of the material doped only with nitrogen and the CDs doped only with gadolinium to compare the impact of each one isolated on the properties and application as an electrocatalyst. Furthermore, the solid CDs obtained by the freeze-drying process were not quantified in terms of mass, i.e., no yield data was presented for the solid CDs, only the volume of the supernatant.

The microwave-assisted synthesis method uses microwave radiation to heat the carbon precursors homogeneously, rapidly, and uniformly, reducing reaction time and improving product yields [225]. It is an advantageous approach due to its simple setup, ease of handling, and control. In addition, as syntheses can be completed in a few minutes, there is a low energy consumption [129]. Huo et al. [226] used the microwave synthesis method to obtain CDs, having citric acid and urea as precursors. The mixture was heated for 5 min at 750 W in a domestic microwave oven. The CDs obtained were used to prepare ZnGa2O4:CDs composites and by microwave synthesis at a fixed temperature of 180 °C for 40 min in a microwave reactor operated at 100 W with dynamic mode (Fig. 7 ).

Pure CDs were obtained from citric acid and urea in microwave heating for 10 min at a power of 1 kW [227]. The obtained CDs solution was mixed with the CdTe:Ce QDs solution to get a Te and Ce RE-CDs composite. Dong et al. [228] obtained CDs modified with europium ions (Eu3+) using microwave (MW) heating in a single step. The reaction between EuCl3·6H2O solution and poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG400) occurred by microwave heating at 200 °C for 20 min. Another composite of RE-CDs was synthesized via microwave by Wang et al. [229]. This study obtained CDs solution by heating tartaric acid, triethylenetetramine, and Co(NO3)2·6H2O for 4 min in a domestic microwave oven operating at 700 W. The CDs were mixed with a Eu(NO3)3·6H2O solution to obtain CDs@Eu-MOFs composites.

Microwave synthesis was recently used to produce a fluorescent sensor with high efficiency by doping it with lanthanum. For this purpose, the carbon dots were first synthesized using a mixture of 10 mL glucose (1 M) and 5 mL sodium citrate (0.5 M). The mixture was heated in a microwave oven at 600 W for 10 min, obtaining a brownish-yellow solution. Subsequently, the carbon dots suspension was mixed with 10 mL lanthanum nitrate (0.3 M) and heated in a microwave oven at 600 W for 6 min. The lanthanum-doped hybrid material with a particle size of 4.8 nm showed a quantum yield of 16 % and blue fluorescence emission when excited at 360 nm. In addition, it showed high sensitivity and selectivity for detecting aniline molecules with a detection limit of 10 nM, within the linear range of 0–2 μM [230].

Europium-doped carbon dots were synthesized via a rapid and simple microwave-mediated method at 300 W for 30 min, using polyethylene glycol as an organic precursor and europium nitrate. The hybrid material showed a narrow and uniform particle size distribution, good photostability, and high photoluminescence quantum yields [231]. Other relevant studies have used the microwave route to synthesize RE-CDs [228,232–234].

Carbonization occurs when carbon precursors are subjected to high temperatures in a controlled environment, such as a pyrolysis furnace [99]. During carbonization, organic molecules undergo breakage and rearrangement and form carbon dots. It is considered cheap and environmentally friendly, especially when it involves the thermal decomposition of low-value-added materials such as agro-industrial organic waste. Using biomass as a carbon source in pyrolysis results in the formation of CDs with a particle size generally between 0.4 nm and 6 nm and a quantum yield of 3 % to 25 % [235]. The synthesis of CDs by the carbonization method is usually carried out in a single step without the need for complicated equipment or processes [236].

In recent years, several strategies have been developed for preparing RE-CDs by direct carbonization. Chen et al. [236] prepared Tb-CDs by a simple direct carbonization method in a thermostatic oil bath at 190 °C for 30 min, using citric acid and Tb(NO3)3·5H2O as precursors (Fig. 8 ). Eu-CDs were prepared based on a pyrolysis method by Rong et al. [237], using Eu(NO3)3·6H2O and citric acid heated at 225 °C for 16 min in a round-bottomed flask.

Fang et al. [238] prepared Ho-CDs composites by low-temperature pyrolysis of citric acid in the presence of HoCl3·6H2O, diethylene triamine pentacetic acid, and polyethylenimine (PEI). The reaction was carried out in an autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon), heating at 180 °C for 2 h. Obtaining RE-CDs by carbonization can also be carried out in more than one step. Li et al. [239] obtained CDs by pyrolysis at 200 °C and 60 min, using citric acid as a carbon source and l-cysteine for surface modification. In a subsequent step, the CDs solution was mixed with Eu(NO3)3, forming the RE-CDs composite.

Europium was used to synthesize a highly selective hybrid material for detecting tetracycline in water samples. Citric acid monohydrate was used as the organic precursor and europium nitrate hexahydrate as the rare earth metal source, applying the direct carbonization method at 200 °C for 5 min. The carbonization products were then dissolved in water, purified by membrane filtration, and freeze-dried. The hybrid formed showed blue fluorescence, with maximum emission at 465 nm, a quantum yield of 4.7 %, excellent chemical stability, and oxidation resistance. In addition, the fluorescence emission of the Eu-CDs was quenched by tetracycline, showing great sensitivity with a limit of detection of 0.3 μM [240].

The synthesis of RE-CDs using the mixing method involves combining previously synthesized CDs with rare earth ions. First, the CDs are synthesized using hydrothermal, solvothermal, and other routes. Subsequently, the CDs are mixed at room temperature with the salt or chemical species containing the rare earth metal, allowing the interaction between them to occur, and thus doping. This results in a hybrid material with superior optical or magnetic properties.

CDs doped with Ce3+, Eu3+, and Tb3+ were synthesized in a process involving several steps. NaGdF4 nanocrystals doped with Ln3+, namely cerium, europium, and terbium, were synthesized and mixed with the previously prepared CDs solution. To do this, the NaGdF4 nanocrystals were first prepared using a mixture of cerium, europium, and terbium nitrate, sodium chloride, ammonium fluoride, and polyethyleneimine, dissolved in ethylene glycol. The solution was transferred to a Teflon-coated stainless-steel autoclave and treated at 200 °C for 2 h. After washing with water and drying, NaGdF4 nanocrystals doped with rare earth were obtained. The CDs were then synthesized using citric acid as a precursor via hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 4 h. Finally, the CDs solution was dripped and added to 0.1 mg/mL of NaGdF4 codoped with Ln3+ ions. Mixing the CDs in the solution with rare earth nanocrystals promoted doping through a mechanism of electrostatic interaction between negatively charged carboxylic groups in the CDs and positively charged amino groups in the nanocrystals [241].

Along the same lines, Wei et al. [242] synthesized NaYF4:Yb,Er@CDs nanocomposites with erbium- and ytterbium-containing CDs in an ultra-fast synthesis of half a minute. Similar to the previous study, the CDs were first synthesized by solvothermal carbonization using citric acid as the carbon source and thiourea as the nitrogen source. Subsequently, NaYF4:Yb,Er was added and mixed with the CDs solution so that adsorption occurred rapidly through electrostatic attraction, impregnating CDs into the structure. The method proved effective in doping and extremely simple, so the process only took place by manual stirring for half a minute at room temperature. However, the quantum yield could have been addressed for comparison purposes to assess the impact on the dual-mode luminescence properties [242].

Zhang et al. [243] used this route to synthesize Eu-CDs, where the CDs were prepared previously (hydrothermal route, 130 °C for 5 h) and then mixed at room temperature in a solution of europium nitrate hexahydrate. The doping mechanism used an imidazole polyionic liquid as a "bridge" molecule to covalently connect CQDs with Eu3+ [243]. Recently, Heng et al. [244] produced a core-shell fluorescent probe CDs@SiO2-Tb for anthrax biomarker detection, using several steps. Firstly, the CDs were prepared hydrothermally at 180 °C for 6 h using a Teflon autoclave. Next, CDs@SiO2 were obtained using reverse microemulsion, followed by functionalization by hydroxylation and carboxylation to obtain CDs@
SiO2-COOH. Finally, the rare earth metal Tb3+ was impregnated through coordination with carboxyl to obtain CDs@SiO2-Tb [244]. Due to the method's practicality, other recent studies have also used this route [221,245–247].

As noted, the favorable results obtained by each method of synthesizing RE-CDs can vary considerably according to the specific synthesis conditions and materials employed. Briefly, Table 1 presents each method's main advantages and disadvantages, giving an overview of the essential considerations for choosing the most suitable way. This information is crucial to guide future research and optimize the synthesis of RE-CDs in different contexts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of RE-CDs: critical analysis and optimization
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the work involving RE-CDs has proven the numerous advantages that these hybrid materials present and their important contribution to science. As discussed in detail, several areas are covered, and many gains have been made with the interaction between RE and CDs. However, there are still limitations and shortcomings in the synthesis processes that could be improved.

For example, there is a lack of standardization in the synthesis process, especially in the purification of the supernatant. There are many differences in the filtration step to obtain the CDs. For example, some studies use filtration through 0.22 µm cellulose acetate membranes, as in studies by Selvaraju et al. [224], while others use filters with an opening of 0.45 µm, as in studies by Abdel-Lateef et al. [209]. Furthermore, others use dialysis, or the purification process is not even mentioned [209,241,243,247]. These differences can impact the final product, especially the size and size distribution of the particles, and thus affect the application [96]. For this reason, standardizing the purification process used in the synthesis of nanoparticles would be the first step in ensuring better homogeneity, uniformity, and quality of the product obtained.

In addition, most studies on doping focus on characterizing the hybrid material and then applying it. However, tests without the dopant for comparative purposes are not commonly carried out. It is essential to address the characterization of CDs without the rare earth element to investigate the impact of the metal's insertion on the material's final properties. The literature lacks such comparative studies regarding absorbance, fluorescence, microscopy, and spectroscopy, among others. Furthermore, it is more important to carry out comparative analyses concerning quantum yield because this variable is related to the optical properties of CDs, allowing a punctual analysis of the influence of RE on luminescence properties.

Another major limitation in the synthesis processes is related to the mass yield of the product, a quantitative aspect that is little addressed. Generally, studies involving the doping of RE-CDs do not present data on the mass yield of the hybrid product produced in terms of initial quantification of the biomass or precursor and final quantification of the nanoparticles obtained for comparative purposes [209]. It is known from experiments that the yield of CDs is very low, and the freeze-drying process to obtain it in powder form is expensive. Oven drying is another way of obtaining powder-form nanoparticles using a more economical route. It is necessary to optimize the obtaining of material for application purposes, such as tracking and marking biofilms, and controlled release systems, among other applications that require the material in solid form [248,249].

Another aspect that deserves more focus in doped CDs studies is using other carbon sources, such as biomass. To date, no studies in the literature thoroughly analyze biomass constituents to investigate what will be converted into CDs in compositional terms. Alternative precursor sources, such as waste, are also good candidates and should be carefully characterized for a comprehensive understanding of the role of different molecules in the synthesis. However, care must be taken independently of the precursor when the process requires treating with strong concentrated acids or high temperatures to ensure carbonization and conversion into CDs [250]. In this case, we suggest carefully evaluating the precursor used in the synthesis and its properties as an initial step.

Furthermore, special care must be taken when synthesizing RE-CDs using fibrous biomass with a large surface area [251]. The rare earth metal can become mostly adsorbed on the biomass and reduce the doping efficiency of the CDs, which is the interest. To get around this, the biomass can be extracted using a suitable solvent and temperature conditions (lower than the carbonization temperature), and the dopant added to the extract. In this way, there is greater assertiveness and a guarantee that the metal will stick to the CDs.

Finally, another aspect that could be implemented to optimize the synthesis is reaction kinetics. Many RE-CDs studies involve long synthesis times, which should be rethought due to the high energy costs [211,252]. It is important to develop research strategies that reconcile the efficiency of the route with the low environmental impact generated. With kinetics, reaction rates can be investigated allowing the researcher to assess how this parameter affects the formation of CDs. It can be investigated how long CDs take to form, so additional reaction hours are unnecessary. This would make the route more economical, as it would optimize the synthesis time. The kinetics can be used to monitor the synthesis in real time, allowing the reaction conditions to be adjusted as necessary, according to the required application. In addition, it can control the reaction rate and allow for the effective conversion of reagents [253]. By knowing the best reaction conditions, they can be applied to the hydrothermal, solvothermal, or pyrolysis carbonization routes, for example. Another way to optimize synthesis is to raise the reaction temperature and reduce the synthesis time [225].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computed tomography and nuclear magnetic resonance
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that the biomedical applications of CDs doped with rare earth have made remarkable advances due to some of the prominent and advantageous characteristics of RE-CDs. These include ease of preparation and modification, high photostability, robust chemical inertness, excellent biocompatibility, and good water solubility [254,255].

However, despite the excellent sensitivity in fluorescent imaging, this sensitivity does not apply to hypotensive areas of the human body. Zhao et al. [51] observed that computed tomography (CT) imaging associated with magnetic resonance imaging promotes an excellent in vitro imaging effect of Gd and Yb doped CDs used as a nanoprobe, stimulating further exploration of its performance in tumor imaging. Zhu et al. [256] explored Gd-doped carbon dots with multi-element charge transfer to detect the malignant bone tumor osteosarcoma. The insertion of characterization with computed tomography and NMR imaging with intense and stable double emission made Gd extremely suitable as a contrast agent for the images generated due to this element's strong X-ray absorption coefficient and half-filled f orbital. Terbium-doped CDs (Tb-CDs) as a new contrast agent for computed tomography were also examined by Molkenova et al. [257]. The CT measurements suggested that Tb-CDs exhibit negligible cytotoxicity towards l-929 cells and demonstrate high X-ray absorption performance. Therefore, these advantages of RE-CDs hybrids can be emphasized regarding biocompatibility and non-toxicity, which are essential characteristics in these areas.

Various spectroscopic techniques, such as ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), infrared spectroscopy (IR), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), dynamic light scattering (DLS), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) have also been used to characterize RE-CDs. IR spectroscopy is commonly used to investigate the composition and functional groups of RE-CDs. This characterization technique has recently been used to investigate the functional groups after doping, making it possible to identify the coordination status of the rare earth ions in the carbon dots surface's carboxyl group and the mechanisms of electron and energy transfers [258].

Zhang et al. [259] and Meng et al. [260] reveal that the stretching vibration detected by FTIR spectroscopy around 1100 cm−1 and 1150 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of the hydroxyl group in the cerium ions, corroborating the doping. In addition, stretching vibrations for the OH single bond at 3395 cm−1 and CO at 1251 cm−1 were detected by FTIR spectroscopy for the doping of carbon dots with cerium, in agreement with studies by Du et al. [261] and Han et al. [262]. The absorbance at 1630 cm−1 is attributed to the NH bending vibration, suggesting the existence of amino-containing functional groups. These peaks suggested that Ce-CDs hybrids contribute positively to hydrophilic properties, stability, and better dispersity [261].

The DLS method is used to hydrodynamically determine the particle size when the RE-CDs are dispersed in a liquid phase. Through this technique, the radii of RE-CDs can be evaluated by measuring the diffusion rate of the nanomaterial in liquefied media. However, the limitation of this method lies in its unreliability and inaccuracy because DLS only provides information on the size distribution of CDs from the complementary function of the photon generated [263–265]. The DLS method can be used in conjunction with other characterization methods to enhance comprehension of particle behavior, particularly concerning stability and size. The DLS technique has also been used in another recent study [237,266].

XPS makes it possible to determine the valence states of elements in carbon materials and the content of these elements [259]. The XPS spectrum suggested the presence of the Gd3+ peak at 140.3 eV in the Gd-CDs with a doping rate of approximately 0.53 %, as reported by Liao et al. [267]. Recently, CDs doped with cerium and nitrogen were synthesized to detect tetracycline residues and bioimaging. Characterization of the nanoparticles by XPS revealed peaks at 285 eV (C 1s), 532 eV (O 1s), 401 eV (N 1s), and 904 eV (Ce 3d), confirming the existence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and cerium. It was also possible to identify that cerium was in the form of Ce3+ and Ce4+ [268]. Dewangan et al. [128] characterized a hybrid material composed of cerium used in the detection of Hg2+ and captopril. The hybrid was composed of C (54.62 %), N (16.0 %), O (28.16 %) and Ce (1.52 %). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum showed three peaks at 282.08 eV, 283.41 eV, and 285.822 eV, referring to the CC/C = C, CN, and CO, COH bonds, respectively. The N 1s spectrum showed peaks at 397.86 eV, 399.01 eV, 400.18 eV, and 403.39 eV, while the O 1s spectrum showed peaks at 531.34 eV, and 529.51 eV. The rare earth metal was identified through the Ce 3d spectrum with two strong peaks at 882.83 eV and 902.75 eV, corresponding to Ce (III). The peak at 880.68 eV, 884.34 eV, 897.72 eV, and 908.32 eV indicated the existence of Ce (IV) [128]. XPS analysis confirmed the presence of cerium, nitrogen, oxygen, and carbon on the material's surface. Other recent studies have also used XPS to characterize RE-CDs [229,230].

Recently, chemical analysis via EDS was used to confirm the presence of Gd in the synthesized CDs, which may be in the form of chelates or doped atoms or ions [252]. The sample consists mainly of C and O, while the contribution of H was neglected due to the lower accuracy of H detection in EDS measurements. Oxygen may belong to surface functional groups. Characterization via EDS was used to determine the chemical identity of gadolinium-doped carbon dots as ratiometric fluorometry sensing [266]. The EDS test determined the composition of the Gd-CDs containing C (4.24 wt%), H (2.31 wt%), N (18.01 wt%), S (0.92 wt%), O (30.56 wt%) and Gd (calculated 43.96 wt%). The advantages of the Gd-CDs hybrid can be seen in its greater sensitivity and good selectivity for detecting morin in human urine samples. EDS analysis was also used to characterize RE-CDs in other recent studies [194,269,270].

Liao et al. [267] report that CDs are mixed nanoparticles when associated with RE, and the variation of fluorescence structures in CDs, when analyzed via UV–Vis spectroscopy, can cause this broad emission property. This statement is corroborated in the study developed by Zheng et al. [269], in which UV–Vis analysis for Gd-CDs revealed a wide emission wavelength range and a typical characteristic dependent on the excitation wavelength. In addition, the maximum emission peak shifted to red in the 380–470 nm range to 330–400 nm as the excitation wavelength varied, emphasizing that Gd-CDs hybrids are endowed with excellent fluorescent properties.

Similarly, the hybrid Eu-CDs were also characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy. A combination of a broad blue-green emission band centered on 500 nm and a red emission, induced by the ligand of the Eu3+ complex [271], was observed. The Eu-CDs showed greater thermal stability and efficient emission of white light after excitation at 400 nm. The characterization results and the technique's important concepts were fundamental for the subsequent manufacture of white light-emitting diodes. Other studies involving the characterization of RE-CDs can be mentioned [51,272].

The morphology and structure of CDs are important attributes in visualizing the arrangement of rare earth elements treated with CDs. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) has been a widely used approach for obtaining topographic images with atomic resolution and mapping certain mechanical and physicochemical properties of RE-CDs. It is important to mention that the dimensions of RE-CDs can be measured by randomly calculating particle heights in 2D images, while 3D images determine the surface morphology of CDs [273].

A study developed by Zhao et al. [51] showed that CDs doped with Gd and Yb exhibit a narrow diameter distribution of 4.0 mm to 8.0 nm. However, in a later study, Gd-CDs with an average size of 3 nm were obtained as contrast agents for magnetic resonance imaging [252]. In addition, cerium-doped carbon dots were also characterized by AFM, where the test determined that the size of the Ce-CDs was approximately 1.5 nm [266]. Despite the variations in particle size, all of these studies meet the important requirement regarding the characteristic particle size of CDs, typically less than 10 nm [194]. Other studies involving the characterization of RE-CDs via AFM can be mentioned [274–276].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is one of the most widely used image characterization techniques for identifying RE-CDs nanoparticles.
