This technique promotes the recovery of chemical information and images of RE-CDs at a spatial resolution to the level of atomic dimensions, providing details on the morphology of the nanoproduct obtained [277]. Thakur et al. [206] used high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) to investigate the morphology and size of the Ce-CDs hybrid applied to the detection of doxorubicin. The nanoparticles were almost spherical, averaging around 50–60 nm. Recently, Jiao et al. [194] obtained Gd-CDs with excellent dispersibility and uniform size distribution with an average diameter of 2.5 nm for fluorescence and cancer phototherapy application. The TEM microscopy technique has been widely used in other recent studies to evaluate the size and shape of nanoparticles [235,241].

Finally, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) can be used as a complementary test to TEM, as in a recent study that used TEM and SEM techniques to determine the size and morphology of Eu-CDs [209]. Characterization by SEM provided details on the spherical shape of the synthesized Eu-CDs, and TEM showed the size of the synthesized Eu-CDs to be around 6 nm. Although less commonly, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) can also be used alone to characterize RE-CDs, as in the studies carried out by Liu et al. [278] and Sur et al. [279]. However, there are some limitations of the technique associated with the low resolution, making it difficult to visualize the morphological characteristics of nanoparticles between 2 nm and 60 nm in size, so another technique can be used.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) allows characterizing the crystal structure, phase purity, and particle size of RE-CDs. Zhang et al. [280] verified via XRD cerium-doped carbon quantum dots applied as a highly efficient antioxidant. Three main peaks at 13.4° (001 plane), 28.7° (002 plane), and a weaker peak at 42.6° (100 plane) showed a multilayer heterogeneous phase Ce-CDs hybrid nanostructure. Studies by Fang et al. [281] confirmed the crystal structure of Gd3+ doped carbon dots using the XDR technique. The Gd-CDs hybrid exhibited weak, broad diffraction peaks at ∼26.2° in the XRD pattern. The diffraction peak at ∼26.2° was assigned to the (002) graphene plane. It indicated the high crystallinity of Gd/CDs since graphene with an ordered crystal structure exhibits a sharp diffraction peak at 26.5° in its XRD pattern. Another example of using XRD to describe the crystal structure of RE-CDs involved a new fluorescent ratiometric probe to determine oxytetracycline based on Eu-CDS [282]. The application of XRD in that study revealed that Eu-CDS hybrids are amorphous with a small weak diffraction peak of ∼26.1°.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of rare earth doped carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Doping carbon dots with cerium (Ce-CD) is an interesting tactic that covers an amalgam of applications, justified by its peculiarities. One of them is the sensitivity in the detection regions, allowing the rare earth element to interact selectively with the analyte obtaining a sensitive and reliable detection. This doping allows assembling fluorescent probes that utilize this specific interaction of the rare earth element with metal ions, making them indispensable in detecting pollutants, toxins, and emerging contaminants [283].

RE-CDs have been applied as highly selective sensors for rapidly and reliably detecting harmful chemical species. A recent study produced CdTe:Ce quantum dots to make a dual fluorescence probe to detect the Hg2+ ion present in tap water [10]. The dual fluorescence probe aims to utilize the fact that the fluorescence of the carbon dots is suppressed in the presence of Hg2+ ions. In contrast, the CdTe:Ce quantum dots' fluorescence is enhanced as a property for detecting the ion. The probe showed a fast and sensitive response to Hg2+, good selectivity with a detection range between 10 nM and 60 nM, and the detection limit was 2.63 nM/L. Following on the detection range, Zhao et al. [284] used Ce-CDs to simultaneously detect Hg2+ and aflatoxin B1 ions in a single analysis, using the SERS (Surface-enhanced Raman Scattering) detection method. The strategy involved preparing Ce-CDs composites doped with gold nanoparticles (Ce-CDs/AuNPs). The presence of Hg2+ in the sample significantly increases the oxygenase-like activity and Raman spectra due to the formation of gold amalgamation (Au@HgNPs). Ce-CDs/AuNPs are prepared using Ce-CDs as reductants, and the added Hg2+ is catalyzed and reduced to Hg0, forming gold amalgam aggregates that cover the surface of Ce-CDs/AuNPs. When AFB1 is introduced into the system, the decrease in SERS signal intensity is observed due to the inhibition of the aggregation of the Au@HgNPs sensors by AFB1 through the carbonyl oxygen atoms. The Hg2+ sensor shows an increase in SERS signal intensity with increasing Hg2+ concentration, while for aflatoxin B1, the SERS signal intensity decreases with increasing AFB1 concentration.

Another reason for using Ce in CDs doping is its applications in agriculture and plant development and improvement studies. Ce-CDs can be absorbed and transported through fibrovascular tissues from roots to stems and leaves, concentrating significantly on plant roots. Cell walls, fibrovascular tissues, leaf veins, and stomata accumulate considerable Ce-CD. This positively impacts increased photosynthesis and resistance to oxidation in plants and favors the development of plant physiological processes. In this regard, a study by Gong et al. [285] investigated Ce-CDs’ effects on photosynthetic and biochemical physiology in controlled wheat crops, observing a positive impact on plant physiological properties.

In addition to detecting harmful chemical species and improving agriculture, Ce-CDs have been applied in the medical field, acting in the detection of potential carcinogenic species. Ce-CDs modified with folic acid (Ce-CD/FA) were used as a photoluminescent sensor for cancer cell identification and Fe(III) detection [286].

Cell identification tests showed Ce-CD/FA had a strong affinity for cancer cells while interacting weakly with healthy cells. The same study also addresses the detection of ions Fe3+ using Ce-CD/FA. The presence of Fe3+ led to the extinction of photoluminescence, allowing sensitive and selective detection of this ion.

Another paper applying Ce-CDs in the biomedical area was developed by Zhang et al. [280], whose objective was to evaluate the antioxidant potential of Ce-CDs in scavenging and reactive oxygen species (ROS). Ce-CDs exhibited excellent free radical scavenging activity, superior to undoped CDs. The free radical scavenging ability was proven by a simple in vitro photometric system, demonstrating the antioxidant efficacy of scavenging hydroxyl radicals. Similarly, Cai et al. [287] also explored the antioxidant potential of Ce-CDs to attenuate oxidative damage induced by ferroptosis, an effect of iron overload in the body, characterized by an iron-dependent lipid peroxidation process, which induces the accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in cells [288]. Ce-CDs showed strong protection of cells against oxidative damage caused by ferroptosis, and the authors suggest that Ce-CDs have the potential as antioxidant drugs to combat oxidative damage.

Besides antioxidant properties, other applications of Ce-CDs involving heavy metal detection, biomedical applications, fluorescent probes, catalysts, etc., have gained notoriety and are summarized in Table 2 .

Carbon dots are applied in several segments due to their luminescent properties, commonly found in the blue or green spectra [294]. However, the fact that light spectra are mainly concentrated in this region limits their application for emissions in different colors, such as red or infrared [178]. The element europium (Eu3+) is an interesting dopant for CDs to extend the spectral range since the lanthanide can emit red light [205,295–297].

To propose a practical and low-cost method, Chang et al. [207] synthesized a composite material of CDs, nitrogen, and hydroxyapatite, containing europium and gadolinium (NCDs/HA:Eu,Gd). Gadolinium (Gd) was added to enhance the red emission of europium (Eu). Under a single excitation wavelength, the NCDs/HA:Eu,Gd composite exhibited dual emission and dual luminescence in both liquid and solid states, which provides an additional advantage. The NCDs/HA:Eu,Gd composite possesses a porous structure, preventing the aggregation of CDs and significantly enhancing UV–vis light absorption. Using the hydrothermal route, nanocomposites of YF3:Eu3+/CDs and YF3:Eu3+,Tb3+/CDs were produced with high potential for application in white LEDs, anti-counterfeiting and solid-state devices [298].

Eu-CDs are also targets of recent research into new fluorescent detector nanomaterials for different analytes. In this perspective, Albalawi et al. [299] synthesized Eu-CDs by hydrothermal carbonization using tannic acid and europium chloride as precursors. The material was developed as a fluorescent reagent to replace synthetic food dyes. Similarly, Abdel-Lateef et al. [209] studied the development of a new fluorescence probe designed to detect the adulteration of curcumin used for therapeutic and biomedical purposes. The probe was synthesized from the carbonization and conversion of tannic acid-Eu3+ complex into bright fluorescence Eu-CDs in the presence of orthophosphoric acid. The fluorescence assay detected metanil yellow, a highly poisonous dye often used in curcumin adulteration. In another study, nanocomposites of CDs@CaF2:Eu3+ were used for the visual determination of Doxycycline as a probe in milk and honey samples with a detection limit of 43 nM [283].

Another reported application for Eu-CDs involves europium photoluminescence applied to optical sensors for metal cations and anions. Lin et al. [300] observed that europium-containing graphene quantum dots (Eu-GQDs) were used as Cu2+ and l-cysteine (L-Cys) tracers with high sensitivity and selectivity. Zhang et al. have also reported the simple and rapid determination of highly toxic and environmentally harmful ions in aquatic environments and organisms, such as Ag+, by Eu-CDs. [301]. Other ionic species such as Fe3+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, CrO2, and Cu2+ are examples of analytes in which Eu-CDs have been employed as sensors/detectors [196,252,259]. To confer new functional properties to microporous polymeric materials, such as luminescent properties, Rybaltovskii et al. [302] developed new photoluminescent materials based on highly porous matrices doped with Eu-CDs resulting in materials with high photocatalytic activity obtained by a low-cost method. The different applications of Eu-CDs are summarized in Table 3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots doped with gadolinium (Gd-CDs) and ytterbium (Yb-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction between Gd-CDs has gained remarkable visibility due to its magnetic resonance properties that make this material suitable for medical applications, particularly for obtaining images with excellent contrast resolution for in vivo diagnosis of various diseases, making the diagnosis faster and non-invasive [314–316].

One of the applications reported in the literature indicates that small magnetofluorescent gadolinium nanoparticles doped with CDs ranging in size from 2 nm to 20 nm can enter the myocardial interstitium through the permeable endothelium and accumulate in ischemic tissues to facilitate imaging of myocardial infarction or serve as a drug carrier to enhance the retention of therapeutic agents in the infarcted myocardium [317]. This hypothesis was verified using a cardiac magnetic resonance imaging contrast agent to detect infarcted myocardium in a myocardial ischemia/reperfusion mice model.

Further studies where the fluorescent properties of the Gd-CDs interaction were applied as an agent for enhanced magnetic resonance imaging and fluorescence imaging of biological tissues were developed by Zheng et al. [269] and Jiao et al. [194]. The researchers applied Gd-CDs synthesized via the solvothermal method as a dual-modal imaging agent for cellular fluorescence and targeted fluorescence-guided photothermal therapy. Maghsoudinia et al. [318] and Huang et al. [319] applied Gd-CDs to detect hepatocellular carcinoma by fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging and to enhance cellular fluorescence imaging from nanocomposites of Gd-CDs and magnetite nanoparticles. Finally, Yao et al. [315] used magnetofluorescent Gd-CDs as a potential diagnostic probe or theranostic agent.

Other applications recently reported in the literature involve the use of Gd-CDs for the identification of biological components. Devi et al. [320] used nitrogen-doped Gd-CDs as electrode material for the sensitive electrochemical detection of the antibiotic clioquinol. The developed sensor possessed high stability, repeatability, and practical reproducibility. Similarly, Wei et al. [198] developed a Gd-CDs sensor for dual pattern recognition of dopamine hydrochloride and d-Glutamic acid, important excitatory neurotransmitters closely related to central nervous system diseases.

Ytterbium-doped Carbon Dots (Yb-CDs) are described in the literature for biomedical applications of cell tracking, photodynamic therapy, and optical diagnostics for presenting, among other characteristics, high atomic number and X-ray absorption potential [321,322]. In addition, as demonstrated in the literature, Yb has a high quantum efficiency in light emission, i.e., a large part of the energy absorbed by the element is converted into a luminescent emission [323]. With this, it is possible to associate the doping of carbon dots with ytterbium with an increase in the emissive intensity of light.

Rahman et al. [324] synthesized ytterbium oxide doped with binary tin oxide in an alkaline phase using the low-temperature hydrothermal technique. The photoluminescent property exhibited by Yb-CDs allowed its application as an electrochemical probe of suitable sensitivity and with long-term stability for Penicillin-G. The study provides a reliable bridge for critical detection applications. Another study by Kumar et al. [325] presents a similar approach of synthesizing Yb-CDs to act as an electrochemical biosensor for dopamine, capable of quantifying the analyte effectively and selectively. The sensor showed excellent signal recovery and reproducibility, with great potential for application in clinical samples for early analysis of neurodegenerative syndromes.

Applications in optoelectronics give more notability to the Yb-CDs product due to its excellent optical and photovoltaic properties [326]. Shi et al. [327] promoted the in situ doping of perovskite with Yb generating a product with enhanced optoelectronic properties, high quantum yield, crystallinity, thermal stability, carrier transport capacity, and energy conversion. The Yb-CDs association exhibited significantly improved storage stability under ambient conditions. Other studies involving the applications and properties of Gd-CDs and Yb-CDs are summarized in Table 4 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots doped with other rare earth elements
ce:section.ce:para: The semiconducting properties of CDs are highly exploited for bioimaging and disease detection. Zhang et al. [332] described a hydrothermal method for preparing lanthanum-doped CDs to be used as an effective fluorescent probe for determining mercury ions in liver epithelial HepG2 cells, exhibiting green and red emission. A similar application is verified by Yang et al. [333] doping La-CDs via microwave pyrolysis to visualize intracellular Fe3+ in live HeLa cells through cell imaging. An important attribute of this study is that even though La3+ is not fluorescent, the emission band changed from blue to green in the fluorescence analysis of the hybrid product. Other studies involving applications of La-CDs can be found: Lakshmi et al. [334], detecting calcium ions in melanoma and plant cells; Wang et al. [335], detecting Fe3+ in colorimetric test paper and anti-counterfeiting information; and Fan et al. [336], detecting tetracyclines in meat based on pH dependence.

Praseodymium is highly valued for its magnetic, electrical, chemical, and optical properties [337]. However, its doping with CDs is not commonly reported, although the co-doping of CDs with cerium and praseodymium and its application as an improved antioxidant for eliminating hydroxyl radicals can be mentioned as an example [338]. Applications of hybrid products of neodymium and CDs are also not commonly reported in recent literature. Alexander et al. [339] produced the paramagnetic hybrid Nd-CDs via the hydrothermal method for application as a nanocarrier for the anticancer drug camptothecin. Another recent application for Nd-CDs involves a new tool for gene therapy against cancer via doping CDs with neodymium and nitrogen, as reported by Valimukhametova et al. [340], where the hybrid formed generated images to detect interfering RNAs.

Among the rare earth elements considered heavy elements, yttrium has fluorescent properties as its main application, especially in ion detection. Zhang et al. [341] used CDs with red fluorescent properties, doped with yttrium via a hydrothermal method. Their application was focused on the sensitive and selective detection of calcium ions. Other examples of the use of hybrid Y-CDs are reported by Wang et al. [342] in atenolol degradation via photocatalytic activity; by Nath et al. [343], for live cell imaging applications; and in the study by Alafeef et al. [344] in the detection of bacteria with low cell density.

Finally, studies involving the generation of RE-CDs hybrids using the element dysprosium can be cited. Atabaev et al. [345] used a facile hydrothermal method to prepare bimodal magneto-fluorescent carbon dots doped with dysprosium. The strong blue-green fluorescence proved remarkable for application to magnetic resonance and fluorescence imaging of living cells. Dysprosium-doped CDs were recently synthesized and characterized for application as a negative contrast agent for magnetic resonance imaging [346]. Additional cell tests were carried out, and the Dy-CDs hybrid product proved to be highly effective in detecting cancer cells and evaluating the efficacy of carbon nitride dots conjugated to doxorubicin. Another application involving the use of Dy-CDs was reported by Li et al. [347] in the production of CDs co-doped with manganese and dysprosium. Using a simple one-step hydrothermal approach, such nanomaterial was used as a fluorescent, magnetic resonance-weighted, four-mode imaging and X-ray computed tomography nanoprobe.

This article discusses the main prospects and challenges for synthesizing and applying rare earth-doped carbon dots (RE-CDs). As discussed throughout this review, CDs have interesting peculiarities that make them a target for research and development of advanced materials. Rare earth elements become promising candidates for doping CDs since these metals offer excellent optical properties, high quantum yields, high electronic density, and discontinuous energy levels resulting from their unique 4f electronic configurations. Therefore, the interaction between these two materials forming the RE-CDs hybrid leaves no doubt about its effectiveness. For this reason, numerous recent studies are making use of this potential and applying RE-CDs hybrid materials in several fields, such as cellular sensing and tracking, photocatalysis, solar cells, ratiometric probes, highly selective sensors, optoelectronic devices, among others discussed throughout this review.

The unique luminescent properties of RE-CDs, due to doping with rare earth ions, offer exciting opportunities for the development of materials with widespread applications. One promising perspective is enhancing the luminescent properties of RE-CDs, such as increasing the quantum efficiency and controlling the emission at different wavelengths.

In addition, synthesizing RE-CDs with uniform and controlled size is a significant challenge that requires more efficient and accurate synthesis methods and large-scale production capability. The application of RE-CDs in optoelectronic devices, such as LEDs and solar cells, shows great potential. However, challenges still need to be overcome, including integrating RE-CDs into complex device architectures and understanding charge and energy transfer mechanisms. In addition, RE-CDs show promise in bioimaging and theranostic applications. However, the development of RE-CDs with high imaging efficiency, low toxicity, and selective targeting of specific cells or tissues is still needed. Incorporating rare earth ions into the CDs may also confer unique catalytic properties, further extending their application capabilities for the degradation of organic pollutants.

The use of RE-CDs as sensors for detecting specific compounds still needs to be improved in the literature. Several compounds, such as reactive oxygen species, metabolites, agrochemicals, and biomarkers, have yet to be widely investigated for their detection using RE-CDs. Furthermore, it is essential to highlight that although works exploring RE-CDs as sensors for pharmaceuticals, hazardous metals, and other emerging pollutants have been identified, studies specifically addressing the use of RE-CDs for pesticide detection have yet to be found or are very rare. Additionally, due to the great concern with detecting agrochemicals in different environments, CDs can be an alternative for rapidly detecting these compounds in other media. This model is advantageous over conventional detection techniques such as High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) and Mass Spectrometry (MS) since the traditional methods have disadvantages such as high procedure time, pretreatment, and complex and expensive instrumentation [348]. Although different CDs have already been reported for analyte and agrochemical detection, rare earth-doped CDs still need to be explored. This gap in the literature suggests an exciting research opportunity to investigate the potential of RE-CDs as sensors for this type of agricultural contaminant.

Another perspective of study gaining more and more relevance is the search for greener and more sustainable methods of RE-CDs synthesis. In this sense, there is a growing interest in developing synthesis processes that involve low energy consumption and use the least possible amount of toxic chemical reagents. This approach aims to reduce the environmental impact and promote the production of RE-CDs in a more economical and environmentally conscious way. The search for greener synthesis methods is a promising direction for advancing studies in RE-CDs and can contribute to their application in several areas. Green synthesis is increasingly encouraged by using carbon precursors from alternative sources such as waste, biomass, lignocellulosic materials, and low-value-added waste, which can serve as a green precursor.

In summary, although we have made significant progress in the synthesis and application of RE-CDs, there are still challenges to be overcome and many applications that need further study. Future advances in this area promise to improve the properties of RE-CDs, opening new application possibilities and driving the development of greener and more sustainable synthesis methods. We hope this work will stimulate discoveries and inspire future studies searching for increasingly advanced materials and applications in rare earth-doped carbon dots.
